section.title: The Twelve and the True Family
section.sectionRationale: The conflict reaches a first climax and a first turning. Jesus heals a withered hand on the Sabbath, and His enemies begin plotting His death. Crowds press from every direction, He appoints the Twelve, and then two shocking verdicts fall: His own family says He is out of His mind, and the scribes say He is possessed. Jesus answers with the parable of the strong man and the warning about blaspheming the Spirit, and redefines His true family as those who do God's will.
section.thesis: Jesus is neither mad nor demonic but the stronger One who has come to bind Satan and plunder his house, and those who receive Him, doing the will of God, become His true family, while those who call the Spirit's work demonic cut themselves off from forgiveness.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the withered hand and the death plot; the crowds and the unclean spirits; the appointing of the Twelve; the charge of madness and the Beelzebul controversy; and the true family of Jesus.

units.label: The withered hand and the death plot
units.note: to do good or to do evil on the Sabbath

verses.text: Once again Jesus entered the synagogue, and a man with a withered hand was there.
In order to accuse Jesus, they were watching to see if He would heal on the Sabbath.
Then Jesus said to the man with the withered hand, “Stand up among us.”
And He asked them, “Which is lawful on the Sabbath: to do good or to do evil, to save life or to destroy it?” But they were silent.
Jesus looked around at them with anger and sorrow at their hardness of heart. Then He said to the man, “Stretch out your hand.” So he stretched it out, and it was restored.
At this, the Pharisees went out and began plotting with the Herodians how they might kill Jesus.

units.label: The crowds and the unclean spirits
units.note: You are the Son of God

verses.text: So Jesus withdrew with His disciples to the sea, accompanied by a large crowd from Galilee, Judea,
Jerusalem, Idumea, the region beyond the Jordan, and the vicinity of Tyre and Sidon. The large crowd came to Him when they heard what great things He was doing.
Jesus asked His disciples to have a boat ready for Him so that the crowd would not crush Him.
For He had healed so many that all who had diseases were pressing forward to touch Him.
And when the unclean spirits saw Him, they fell down before Him and cried out, “You are the Son of God!”
But He warned them sternly not to make Him known.

units.label: The appointing of the Twelve
units.note: to be with Him and to be sent out

verses.text: Then Jesus went up on the mountain and called for those He wanted, and they came to Him.
He appointed twelve of them, whom He designated as {{apostolos|apostles}}, to accompany Him, to be sent out to preach,
and to have authority to drive out demons.
These are the twelve He appointed: Simon (whom He named Peter),
James son of Zebedee and his brother John (whom He named Boanerges, meaning “Sons of Thunder”),
Andrew, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Thaddaeus, Simon the Zealot,
and Judas Iscariot, who betrayed Jesus.

units.label: Madness or the strong man bound
units.note: how can Satan drive out Satan

verses.text: Then Jesus went home, and once again a crowd gathered, so that He and His disciples could not even eat.
When His family heard about this, they went out to take custody of Him, saying, “He is out of His mind.”
And the scribes who had come down from Jerusalem were saying, “He is possessed by Beelzebul,” and, “By the prince of the demons He drives out demons.”
So Jesus called them together and began to speak to them in parables: “How can Satan drive out Satan?
If a kingdom is divided against itself, it cannot stand.
If a house is divided against itself, it cannot stand.
And if Satan is divided and rises against himself, he cannot stand; his end has come.
Indeed, no one can enter a {{ischyros|strong man}}’s house to steal his possessions unless he first ties up the strong man. Then he can plunder his house.
Truly I tell you, the sons of men will be forgiven all sins and blasphemies, as many as they utter.
But whoever {{blasphemia|blasphemes}} against the Holy Spirit will never be forgiven; he is guilty of eternal sin.”
Jesus made this statement because they were saying, “He has an unclean spirit.”

units.label: The true family of Jesus
units.note: whoever does the will of God

verses.text: Then Jesus’ mother and brothers came and stood outside. They sent someone in to summon Him,
and a crowd was sitting around Him. “Look,” He was told, “Your mother and brothers are outside, asking for You.”
But Jesus replied, “Who are My mother and My brothers?”
Looking at those seated in a circle around Him, He said, “Here are My mother and My brothers!
For whoever does the {{thelema|will}} of God is My brother and sister and mother.”

apostolos.gloss: One sent, an envoy or authorized representative. Jesus appoints twelve to be with Him and then to be sent out with His own authority to preach and cast out demons. The number twelve deliberately echoes the twelve tribes, marking the reconstitution of God's people around Jesus.

ischyros.gloss: Strong, mighty, powerful. The strong man is Satan, secure in his house until a stronger One comes. Jesus' exorcisms are not collusion with the devil but the plundering of his house, proof that the stronger One has arrived to bind him.

blasphemia.gloss: Slander, reviling, blasphemy, especially speech that insults God. To blaspheme the Holy Spirit is to look at the Spirit's undeniable work in Jesus and call it the devil's, a settled, hardened rejection of the only One who brings forgiveness, and so it cannot be forgiven.

thelema.gloss: Will, desire, purpose. Doing the will of God, receiving and following Jesus, is what marks out His true family. Kinship in the kingdom is defined not by blood but by glad obedience to the Father.

basic.context: Jesus heals a man's withered hand on the Sabbath, exposing the leaders' hard hearts, and they immediately begin plotting to kill Him. Crowds swarm Him from every region. He goes up a mountain and appoints twelve apostles to be with Him and to be sent out. Then two verdicts land: His own family think He has lost His mind, and the Jerusalem scribes say He is driving out demons by the devil. Jesus shows the charge is absurd, He is the stronger One binding Satan, warns about blaspheming the Spirit, and says His real family is whoever does God's will.
basic.hermeneutics: Notice the pattern of insiders and outsiders. The crowds, the family, and the scribes all misread Jesus, while the Twelve are drawn close and the circle of hearers becomes His family. The warning about the unforgivable sin is not about a careless word but a settled, defiant rejection that calls God's Spirit satanic.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus appoints the twelve apostolos, sent ones, to represent Him. He is the stronger One who ties up the ischyros, the strong man, Satan, and plunders his house. To call His work demonic is to blasphemia, blaspheme, the Holy Spirit. And His true family are those who do the thelema, the will, of God.
basic.typology: The twelve apostles correspond to the twelve tribes of Israel; Jesus is founding a renewed people of God around Himself. Binding the strong man fulfils the promise that the seed of the woman would crush the serpent, and looks ahead to the final defeat of Satan.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Lord of the Sabbath who does good on it, the stronger One who binds Satan, the head of a renewed people, and the one whose family is defined by obedience to God. Even the demons confess Him the Son of God.
basic.god: God's will is the dividing line: those who do it belong to Jesus. God's Spirit is at work in Jesus' every deliverance, so to name that work satanic is to set oneself against God Himself.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 3:15 promises the crushing of the serpent. Isaiah 49:24-25 speaks of plunder taken from the mighty. Hebrews 2:14 says Jesus came to destroy the devil.
basic.oneStory: The war declared in Eden reaches a decisive stage. The stronger One has come to bind the strong man and plunder his house, gathering a renewed Israel around Himself, twelve apostles for twelve tribes. Membership in this new family is open to all who do the will of God, and the story presses on toward the cross where Satan's house is finally overthrown.

context.literary: The withered hand completes the collection of five conflict stories begun in chapter 2, ending with a death plot, Mark 3:6, a hinge in the Gospel. Mark then uses a sandwich: the family's approach in verse 21 is interrupted by the scribes' charge in verses 22 to 30, and resumed in verses 31 to 35, so the two misjudgements interpret each other.
context.historical: The Pharisees and Herodians were natural enemies, yet joined against Jesus, showing how threatening He had become. Beelzebul was a name for Satan or the prince of demons. Appointing twelve carried unmistakable national symbolism, evoking the twelve tribes and the hope of a restored Israel.
context.canonical: The binding of the strong man draws on the promise of Genesis 3:15 and the prophetic hope of plunder wrested from the mighty, Isaiah 49:24-25. The Twelve become the foundation of the church, Ephesians 2:20 and Revelation 21:14. The family redefinition anticipates the new-birth family of God, John 1:12-13.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jesus as the stronger One who has come to bind Satan and gather a renewed people, and to expose the tragic misjudgements of Him as madman and as demon-possessed, defining true kinship with Him by obedience to God rather than by blood or religious status.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The appointment of the Twelve is a unique, foundational act, not a template for church offices in every detail. What is prescriptive is the call to do the will of God as the mark of belonging to Jesus, and the sober warning against hardened rejection of the Spirit's work.

debates.question: What is the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit that will never be forgiven?

views.label: Attributing the Spirit's work to Satan
views.case: In context, verse 30, the scribes were calling the Spirit-empowered deliverances demonic; the unforgivable sin is this deliberate, clear-eyed identification of God's Spirit as the devil, a total, settled rejection of the only source of pardon.

views.label: Final, unrepentant hardness of heart
views.case: The sin is unforgivable not because God lacks mercy but because such a hardened stance never seeks forgiveness; the person who fears having committed it, and wants to repent, shows they have not.

debates.bearing: Both readings comfort the troubled conscience: the sin is a persistent, defiant rejection of the Spirit's testimony to Christ, not an isolated word; anyone longing for mercy may freely have it.

debates.question: Why does Jesus seem to distance Himself from His mother and brothers?

views.label: Redefining family around obedience, not blood
views.case: Jesus is not disowning His family but teaching that kinship in the kingdom is by doing God's will; natural ties do not secure a place, and the door stands open to all who obey.

views.label: A gentle rebuke of their attempt to restrain Him
views.case: The family had come to take custody of Him, thinking Him mad, verse 21; Jesus lovingly corrects their misunderstanding while pointing to the wider family gathered around Him.

debates.bearing: Either way the point is that belonging to Jesus is defined by faith and obedience, a family open to everyone, including, later, His own believing mother and brothers, Acts 1:14.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus appoints twelve apostolos, sent ones commissioned to bear His authority, a deliberate echo of the twelve tribes and the reconstitution of Israel around Himself. Against the charge that He casts out demons by the devil, He points to the ischyros, the strong man, Satan, whose house can be plundered only by one stronger, and He is that stronger One. To look at His Spirit-empowered deliverances and call them satanic is to blasphemia, to blaspheme, the Holy Spirit, a hardened rejection that forecloses forgiveness. And His true family are those who do the thelema, the will, of God, a kinship of grace open to all.

typology.fulfillment: The renewed people of God, corresponding to the twelve tribes of Israel and becoming the foundation of the church, Ephesians 2:20 and Revelation 21:14.

typology.type: Binding the strong man and plundering his house
typology.fulfillment: Christ overpowering Satan, promised in Genesis 3:15 and accomplished at the cross, Colossians 2:15 and Hebrews 2:14.

typology.type: The family defined by God's will
typology.fulfillment: The new family of God born not of blood but of God, John 1:12-13 and Galatians 3:26-29.

study.christ: Jesus stands revealed as the Lord who does good on the Sabbath even as His enemies plot His death, the stronger One who has invaded Satan's house to bind him and set his captives free, and the founder of a renewed Israel in the Twelve. The demons themselves fall down and confess Him the Son of God, though He silences them. Neither the crowd's fascination, nor His family's fear that He is mad, nor the scribes' slander that He is demonic can touch the truth of who He is. And He opens His own family to all who do the will of God, the family He will secure by the cross where the strong man is finally overthrown.
study.god: God is at work in every deliverance Jesus performs, so that to call this work the devil's is to blaspheme the Spirit of God. God's will becomes the defining mark of Jesus' true family: not blood, not nationality, not religious pedigree, but glad obedience to the Father. And God's ancient purpose to crush the serpent advances here as the stronger One binds Satan, showing that the living God is reclaiming His world from the powers that have held it captive.

crossReferences.note: The promise that the seed of the woman would crush the serpent, advanced as Jesus binds the strong man.
Can plunder be taken from the mighty? God promises to contend and rescue the captives, fulfilled in Jesus.
Jesus shared in flesh and blood to destroy through death the one who holds the power of death, the devil.
To all who received Him He gave the right to become children of God, born of God, the family defined by faith.

study.oneStory: The conflict that began in Eden comes to a head. The stronger One has entered the strong man's house to bind him and carry off his goods, and around Himself He gathers a renewed people, twelve apostles for the twelve tribes. The tragedy is that His own family and the religious experts misread Him as mad or demonic, while the door to His true family swings open to anyone who does the will of God. The plot to kill Him, hatched here, will become the very means by which Satan's house is plundered for good.

quiz.q: What did Jesus expose by healing the withered hand on the Sabbath?

options.text: That doing good and saving life is what the Sabbath is for, while His opponents' hard hearts preferred to plot evil.
options.note: Mark 3:4-6 sets Jesus' question about doing good against the leaders' murderous hardness.

options.text: That He was willing to work on the Sabbath.
options.note: He did heal on the Sabbath, but the deeper exposure was the leaders' hardness and His teaching that the day is for doing good.

options.text: That the man's faith was greater than the crowd's.
options.note: The focus is not the man's faith but the lawfulness of doing good and the leaders' hearts.

options.text: That the Sabbath law had never mattered to God.
options.note: Jesus upholds the Sabbath's true purpose rather than dismissing it.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus appoint twelve apostles?

options.text: To be with Him and to be sent out with His authority, forming a renewed people echoing the twelve tribes.
options.note: Mark 3:14-15 states their purpose; the number twelve signals a reconstituted Israel.

options.text: To be with Him and to be sent out to preach and cast out demons.
options.note: Correct about their commission, but it misses the deeper significance of the number twelve for a renewed Israel.

options.text: To manage the crowds that pressed around Him.
options.note: A boat handled the crowds; the Twelve were appointed for mission, not crowd control.

options.text: To replace the priests in the temple.
options.note: The Twelve were sent to preach and deliver, not to serve as temple priests.

quiz.q: How does Jesus answer the charge that He casts out demons by the devil?

options.text: Satan cannot drive out Satan; rather, Jesus is the stronger One who binds the strong man and plunders his house.
options.note: Mark 3:23-27 gives both the logic and the strong-man image.

options.text: A kingdom divided against itself cannot stand.
options.note: True and part of His answer, but incomplete without the claim to be the stronger One binding Satan.

options.text: He simply denied doing any exorcisms.
options.note: He does not deny the exorcisms; He explains their true source and meaning.

options.text: He admitted using Beelzebul's power for good ends.
options.note: He flatly rejects the charge; His power is the Spirit's, not the devil's.

quiz.q: What is the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit in this passage?

options.text: A settled, defiant labelling of the Spirit's work in Jesus as demonic, a hardened rejection of the only source of forgiveness.
options.note: Verse 30 ties it to their claim that He has an unclean spirit; it is a hardened rejection, not a single slip.

options.text: Speaking any careless word against the Holy Spirit.
options.note: It is more than a careless word; it is a persistent, clear-eyed rejection of the Spirit's testimony to Christ.

options.text: Doubting whether Jesus really performed miracles.
options.note: Honest doubt is not the unforgivable sin; the sin is calling the Spirit's evident work satanic.

options.text: Any sin committed after conversion.
options.note: Jesus says all other sins and blasphemies can be forgiven; this is a specific, hardened rejection.

quiz.q: Who does Jesus say are His true mother and brothers?

options.text: Whoever does the will of God is His brother and sister and mother.
options.note: Mark 3:35 defines His true family by obedience to God.

options.text: Those who sit near Him and listen to His teaching.
options.note: He gestures to the listeners, but the defining mark He names is doing God's will, not mere proximity.

options.text: Only His biological mother and brothers from Nazareth.
options.note: Jesus deliberately widens the family beyond blood ties to all who do God's will.

options.text: Only the twelve apostles.
options.note: The family is open to whoever does God's will, not the Twelve alone.

questions: Jesus was grieved at hard hearts that would rather plot than rejoice at a healing. Where might your own heart be hardening toward what God is doing?
The Twelve were appointed first to be with Jesus and then to be sent. How does being with Him shape and fuel the ways you are sent into the world?
The scribes saw undeniable good and called it evil. How can you guard against a cynicism that explains away the clear work of God?
Jesus binds the strong man and plunders his house. How does knowing that Satan is already being overpowered change the way you face spiritual struggle?
Jesus' true family is defined by doing God's will. What would it look like this week to belong to Jesus not by label but by glad obedience?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts do not include the phrase whom He designated as apostles; the meaning is unaffected, since the Twelve are plainly sent out with Jesus' authority.
His family, literally those with Him or His own people, went out to take custody of Him, convinced He was beside Himself; Mark frames this alongside the scribes' harsher charge.
Guilty of eternal sin translates a phrase meaning liable to a sin that has no end of guilt; the context, verse 30, defines it as calling the Holy Spirit's work an unclean spirit's.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 3:1-35. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
